title: Benches that face each other, or away?
summary: Argue how a public square should arrange its benches — facing each other to invite conversation, or facing outward for privacy — using present perfect, conditionals, and concession.
topic: Should a redesigned town square place its benches facing one another or facing outward?
prompt: A small city is redesigning its central square and has asked residents how the benches should be arranged. Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether the benches should face each other, encouraging strangers to talk, or face outward toward the view, protecting people who want to sit alone. Use a real reason for each side, then take a clear position. Use present perfect, a conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once each.
hintStarters: I have spent many afternoons watching how people choose where to sit, and I have noticed that…
Although benches that face each other can spark a friendly conversation, they can also…
If the square offered both kinds of seating instead of only one, then…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một cuộc trò chuyện thân mật
một cách bố trí mời gọi
thiết kế cho tất cả mọi người
một quy tắc bất thành văn

sampleText:
For three years I have eaten lunch in the same square in the centre of Porto, and I have learned that the shape of a bench decides who sits there. The old benches near the fountain face each other across a narrow path, and they are usually full of older men playing cards and arguing about football. The newer benches along the south wall face outward toward the river, and the people there sit alone, reading or simply looking at the water. Nobody arranged the city this way on purpose. People found the seating that matched the mood they arrived with, and the benches quietly sorted them.

There is a strong argument for benches that face each other. A square is one of the few places left where strangers from different backgrounds share the same air without paying for anything, and seating that faces inward gives them a reason to speak. I have watched a tourist ask an old man for directions across two facing benches, and twenty minutes later they were still talking. If every bench faced outward, that conversation would never have started, because outward seating sends a clear and silent message: please do not disturb me. A city that only offers private seating slowly teaches its residents that other people are an interruption rather than a resource.

Although I value those accidental conversations, I do not think the whole square should force them. People come to a public square in very different states of mind. Someone who has just lost a job, or who needs ten quiet minutes before returning to a noisy office, has the same right to the square as the cheerful card players. If the council were to make every bench face inward, it would make solitude impossible in the one outdoor room the city shares, and many people would simply stop coming. The best squares I have seen do not choose; they keep both kinds of seating and let the visitor decide. The facing benches near the water, and the outward benches in the shade, should both survive the redesign. A good square is not one that decides how its people should feel. It is one that has enough variety to hold all of them on the same afternoon, the talkative and the silent, side by side without conflict. The real test of a public space is whether a lonely person and a sociable person can both leave it feeling that the city made room for them.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for experience built up over time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'For three years I have eaten lunch in the same square… and I have learned that…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả một thói quen và sự hiểu biết tích lũy qua thời gian, vẫn còn đúng ở hiện tại. 'For three years' chỉ khoảng thời gian kéo dài đến nay.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'For three years I have eaten lunch in the same square… and I have learned that…' uses the present perfect to describe a habit and an understanding built up over time and still true now. 'For three years' marks a span reaching up to the present.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to imagine a different past outcome
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If every bench faced outward, that conversation would never have started' kết hợp giả định để nói về một kết quả khác trong quá khứ — cuộc trò chuyện đã thực sự xảy ra, nên 'would never have started' diễn tả điều ngược lại với thực tế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If every bench faced outward, that conversation would never have started' uses a conditional to imagine a different past outcome — the conversation really did happen, so 'would never have started' expresses the opposite of what was true.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong ba năm, tôi đã ăn trưa ở cùng một quảng trường ở trung tâm Porto, và tôi đã học được rằng hình dạng của một chiếc ghế quyết định ai sẽ ngồi đó. Những chiếc ghế cũ gần đài phun nước quay mặt vào nhau qua một lối đi hẹp, và chúng thường đầy những người đàn ông lớn tuổi chơi bài và cãi nhau về bóng đá. Những chiếc ghế mới hơn dọc bức tường phía nam quay ra ngoài hướng về phía dòng sông, và những người ở đó ngồi một mình, đọc sách hoặc chỉ nhìn dòng nước. Không ai sắp xếp thành phố theo cách này một cách có chủ đích. Mọi người tìm thấy chỗ ngồi phù hợp với tâm trạng mình mang đến, và những chiếc ghế lặng lẽ phân loại họ.

Có một lập luận mạnh mẽ ủng hộ những chiếc ghế quay mặt vào nhau. Một quảng trường là một trong số ít nơi còn lại nơi những người xa lạ từ các hoàn cảnh khác nhau cùng hít chung một bầu không khí mà không phải trả tiền cho bất cứ thứ gì, và chỗ ngồi quay vào trong cho họ một lý do để nói chuyện. Tôi đã chứng kiến một du khách hỏi đường một ông già qua hai chiếc ghế quay vào nhau, và hai mươi phút sau họ vẫn còn đang trò chuyện. Nếu mọi chiếc ghế đều quay ra ngoài, cuộc trò chuyện đó sẽ không bao giờ bắt đầu, bởi vì chỗ ngồi quay ra ngoài gửi đi một thông điệp rõ ràng và lặng lẽ: xin đừng làm phiền tôi. Một thành phố chỉ cung cấp chỗ ngồi riêng tư dần dần dạy cư dân của nó rằng người khác là sự quấy rầy chứ không phải một nguồn lực.

Mặc dù tôi trân trọng những cuộc trò chuyện tình cờ đó, tôi không nghĩ cả quảng trường nên ép buộc chúng. Mọi người đến một quảng trường công cộng với những trạng thái tâm lý rất khác nhau. Một người vừa mất việc, hoặc cần mười phút yên tĩnh trước khi quay lại văn phòng ồn ào, có quyền với quảng trường ngang bằng những người chơi bài vui vẻ. Nếu hội đồng làm cho mọi chiếc ghế đều quay vào trong, nó sẽ khiến sự cô đơn riêng tư trở nên bất khả thi trong căn phòng ngoài trời duy nhất mà thành phố cùng chia sẻ, và nhiều người sẽ đơn giản là ngừng đến. Những quảng trường tốt nhất tôi từng thấy không lựa chọn; chúng giữ cả hai loại chỗ ngồi và để khách quyết định. Những chiếc ghế quay vào nhau gần mặt nước, và những chiếc ghế quay ra ngoài trong bóng râm, đều nên tồn tại qua đợt thiết kế lại. Một quảng trường tốt không phải là nơi quyết định người dân của nó nên cảm thấy thế nào. Đó là nơi có đủ sự đa dạng để chứa tất cả họ trong cùng một buổi chiều, người hay nói và người im lặng, cạnh nhau mà không xung đột. Phép thử thực sự của một không gian công cộng là liệu một người cô đơn và một người hòa đồng đều có thể rời khỏi đó với cảm giác rằng thành phố đã dành chỗ cho họ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe a habit continuing up to now?
options: For three years I have eaten lunch in the same square.
For three years I eat lunch in the same square.
For three years I have ate lunch in the same square.
Since three years I have eaten lunch in the same square.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have eaten' with 'for + period' describes a habit reaching up to now. Simple present 'eat' loses the connection to the past. 'Have ate' uses the wrong past participle. 'Since' is used with a point in time, not a length of time.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'for + length of time' with the present perfect, and the correct past participle of 'eat'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede a point?
options: Although I value those conversations, but I do not think the square should force them.
Although I value those conversations, I do not think the square should force them.
Although I value those conversations I do not think the square should force them.
I value those conversations, although but the square should not force them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' takes no extra 'but' and uses a comma between the clauses. Option B doubles the connector. Option C drops the comma, hurting clarity. Option D uses 'although' and 'but' together.
mcQuestions.hint: Do not pair 'although' with 'but', and put a comma between the two clauses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is the most natural way to say seating sends a 'do not disturb' signal?
options: Outward seating sends a clear and silence message.
Outward seating throws a clear and silent message.
Outward seating sends a clear and silent message.
Outward seating sends a clearly and silent message.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Clear and silent' are both adjectives modifying 'message', and we 'send a message'. Option B uses the noun 'silence' where an adjective is needed. Option C uses 'throws', which does not collocate with 'message'. Option D mixes an adverb 'clearly' with the adjective 'silent'.
mcQuestions.hint: You need two adjectives before 'message', and the verb that collocates with 'message'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a conditional correctly to imagine a different past?
options: If every bench faced outward, that conversation will never have started.
If every bench would face outward, that conversation would never have started.
If every bench faced outward, that conversation would never started.
If every bench faced outward, that conversation would never have started.
mcQuestions.explanation: The if-clause sets a hypothetical condition and 'would never have started' describes the imagined past result. Option B uses 'will', which is not used for hypothetical results. Option C puts 'would' in the if-clause, which is non-standard. Option D drops 'have', breaking the perfect form.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep 'would' out of the if-clause and keep 'have' in the result clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes people from different lives sharing a space for free?
options: strangers from different backgrounds share the same air
strangers from different backgrounds divide the same air
strangers from different backgrounds share the same airs
strangers of different backgrounds shares the same air
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Share the same air' is the natural idiom and the verb agrees with the plural subject. Option B uses 'divide', which changes the meaning. Option C wrongly pluralises 'air'. Option D uses 'shares' with a plural subject.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Air' is uncountable here, and the verb must agree with 'strangers'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'have the same right to' with a comparison?
options: She has the same right of the square as the card players.
She has the same right to the square as the card players.
She has the same right to the square like the card players.
She has same right to the square as the card players.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The same right to X as Y' is the correct pattern. Option B uses 'of' instead of 'to'. Option C uses 'like' instead of 'as' in a comparison of equality. Option D drops the article 'the'.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed pattern is 'the same … to … as …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'enough' correctly before a noun?
options: The square has variety enough to holding all of them.
The square has enough of variety to hold all of them.
The square has enough variety to hold all of them.
The square has enough variety for hold all of them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Enough + noun + to + infinitive' is the standard pattern. Option B uses 'holding' after 'to'. Option C inserts an unnecessary 'of'. Option D uses 'for' where 'to' is needed before the infinitive.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Enough' goes before the noun, and 'to' is followed by the base verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally contrasts two ways of seeing other people?
options: Other people are an interruption rather a resource.
Other people are an interruption instead a resource.
Other people are more an interruption than a resource to be.
Other people are an interruption rather than a resource.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A rather than B' is the clean contrast structure. Option B drops 'than'. Option C uses 'instead' without 'of'. Option D is wordy and ends awkwardly with 'to be'.
mcQuestions.hint: The natural contrast pattern is 'A rather than B'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly describes the square as the only outdoor room shared by the city?
options: the one outdoor room the city shares
the one outdoor room that the city share
the one outdoor room which the city sharing
the one outdoor room the city is share
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The one outdoor room (that) the city shares' uses a correct relative clause with subject–verb agreement. Option B uses 'share' instead of 'shares'. Option C uses 'sharing' without an auxiliary. Option D uses 'is share', which is not a valid verb form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the writer's final test for a good public space?
options: The real test is if a lonely person and a sociable person can both to leave it feeling welcome.
The real test is whether a lonely person and a sociable person can both leave it feeling welcome.
The real test is whether a lonely person and a sociable person can both leaving it feeling welcome.
The real test is whether a lonely person and a sociable person both can leaves it feeling welcome.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether … can both leave …' uses the bare infinitive after 'can' and reads naturally. Option B inserts 'to' after 'can'. Option C uses 'leaving' after 'can'. Option D uses 'leaves' after 'can', which must take the base form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'were to' for a formal hypothetical recommendation?
options: If the council was to make every bench face inward, solitude would become impossible.
If the council were to making every bench face inward, solitude would become impossible.
If the council were to make every bench face inward, solitude would become impossible.
If the council were make every bench face inward, solitude would become impossible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were to + base verb' is the formal hypothetical form. Option B uses informal 'was to'. Option C uses 'making' after 'to'. Option D drops 'to' after 'were'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'were to + base verb' for the formal second conditional.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer claims that a city which only offers private seating slowly teaches people to see strangers as an interruption. Do you think the design of everyday spaces really shapes how we treat one another, or do we behave the same way regardless of how a place is built?
